coredata.dc:description: The convenience and high efficiency of recently developed I–III–VI group AgInS2 (AIS) fluorescence sensors have garnered considerable attention. In this study, glutathione (GSH) was employed as a stabilizer to synthesize Mn doped AgInS2 quantum dots (Mn-AIS QDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method at a lower temperature. The resultant samples displayed favorable photoluminescent characteristics and excellent water dispersibility. The photoluminescence of Mn-AIS QDs is quenched by Fe (III) via a photo-induced electron transfer mechanism (PET), and this quenching can be reversed by ascorbic acid (AA) as a result of the redox reaction between the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) complex and AA. Utilizing the on–off–on fluorescence principle, a fluorescence switch sensor based on Mn-AIS QDs was developed for the detection of Fe (III) and AA. The linear range for the detection of Fe (III) using the Mn-AIS QDs sensor was established to be 0.03–120 µM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.16 nM. For the detection of AA within the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system, the linear range spanned from 0.05 to 180 µM, with a LOD of 0.031 µM. Both Mn-AIS and Mn-AIS-Fe (III) demonstrated robust anti-interference properties, facilitating the accurate detection of Fe (III) in tap water and AA in vitamin C tablets. This approach is notable for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and considerable potential for application in the creation of innovative biological and environmental sensors.

head.ce:title: Construction of a fluorescence switch sensor of Mn doped AgInS2 quantum dots for the detection of Fe (III) and ascorbic acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The convenience and high efficiency of recently developed I–III–VI group AgInS2 (AIS) fluorescence sensors have garnered considerable attention. In this study, glutathione (GSH) was employed as a stabilizer to synthesize Mn doped AgInS2 quantum dots (Mn-AIS QDs) via a one-step hydrothermal method at a lower temperature. The resultant samples displayed favorable photoluminescent characteristics and excellent water dispersibility. The photoluminescence of Mn-AIS QDs is quenched by Fe (III) via a photo-induced electron transfer mechanism (PET), and this quenching can be reversed by ascorbic acid (AA) as a result of the redox reaction between the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) complex and AA. Utilizing the on–off–on fluorescence principle, a fluorescence switch sensor based on Mn-AIS QDs was developed for the detection of Fe (III) and AA. The linear range for the detection of Fe (III) using the Mn-AIS QDs sensor was established to be 0.03–120 µM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.16 nM. For the detection of AA within the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system, the linear range spanned from 0.05 to 180 µM, with a LOD of 0.031 µM. Both Mn-AIS and Mn-AIS-Fe (III) demonstrated robust anti-interference properties, facilitating the accurate detection of Fe (III) in tap water and AA in vitamin C tablets. This approach is notable for its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and considerable potential for application in the creation of innovative biological and environmental sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detection has become an important optical detection technology in the fields of environment, medicine, and data security due to its simplicity, high sensitivity, and high stability [1,2]. Various fluorescent switches have been developed by controlling the “on-off” of fluorescence. These switches are used for detecting many metal ions and biomolecules that play crucial roles in biological systems or environmental processes. As a result, the exploration of effective and selective combinations of fluorescent switches has garnered significant experimental and theoretical attention.

In recent years, there have been developments in fluorescence systems based on emerging I–III–VI ternary quantum dots (QDs). Most of these strategies rely on fluorescence “on-off” mechanisms, which involve the use of intermediates to quench photoluminescence and biological thiols to restore fluorescence. However, the intermediates used are typically heavy metal ions such as Hg (II) [3,4], Ag (I) [5,6], Cu (II) [7,8] and Au (III) [9], posing significant environmental and public health concerns. Therefore, it is crucial to explore alternative ions, such as Fe (III), for this application-driven detection platform. Detection of Fe (III) is particularly important as it is essential for various biological systems, and its deficiency can lead to diseases such as anemia, renal failure, and liver damage [10,11]. Currently, methods for detecting metal ions primarily include ultraviolet spectrophotometry [12], atomic absorption spectroscopy [13], atomic fluorescence spectrometry [14], X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [15], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [16]. Other new technologies, such as neutron activation analysis [17] and electrochemical method [18] have also been applied for the detection of extremely trace heavy metal ions. While these methods enable the detection of heavy metal ions, they often suffer from drawbacks such as expensive equipment, complex procedures, high technical requirements, and long detection times. Therefore, it is crucial to explore convenient and rapid methods for detecting heavy metal ions. Among various detection strategies, fluorescence-based methods have gained increased attention due to their low cost, ease of operation, and visualization capabilities in detecting heavy metal ions.

Ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, serves as a vital antioxidant with a crucial role in maintaining a well-balanced diet for human beings. Its wide-ranging applications encompass the prevention and treatment of diverse diseases, including but not limited to colds, psychosis, infertility, AIDS, and cancer. Consequently, there is substantial importance in achieving sensitive and selective detection of AA in industries such as environmental monitoring, pharmaceuticals, and food production. Presently, commonly employed techniques for AA detection comprise enzyme analysis [19], chromatography [20], electrochemical analysis [21,22], colorimetry [23], and fluorescence analysis [24]. Out of these methods, fluorescence analysis stands out due to its exceptional attributes including high sensitivity, broad response linear range, and low detection limit.

I–III–VI multi-element QDs exhibit excellent physical and chemical properties, including high fluorescence emission, long fluorescence lifetime, broad excitation band, narrow emission band, large Stokes shift, strong resistance to photobleaching and environmental friendliness. By adjusting the chemical composition, the emission range of these QDs can be continuously tuned from the visible to near-infrared region, avoiding the use of heavy metals such as Cd, Hg, and Pb. These advantages make them ideal materials for replacing traditional binary QDs, leading to their wide application prospects in fields such as solar cells [25], light-emitting diodes [26], sensors [27], and biological imaging [28]. However, compared to binary QDs, multi-element QDs containing various metal ions have different reaction rates between metal ions and ligands, resulting in more internal defects and increased susceptibility to fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and stability of these QDs need to be improved. Doping transition metal ions (such as Zn2+, Mn2+ or Cu+) can effectively regulate the band gap width of QDs, increase the Stokes shift, and promote radiation recombination, thereby broadening the luminescence range and improving the quantum yield. AgInS2 (AIS) is a new type of I–III–VI nanomaterials with good biocompatibility, eco-friendliness, excellent photonics and high photostability. It is widely used in chemical sensing, biological imaging and other fields. However, the significant difference in reactivity between Ag+ and In3+ with S2− can lead to the formation of defects within the crystal structure, thereby impacting its optical properties. To mitigate this issue, the incorporation of organic ligands and transition metal ions is employed to enhance the fluorescence properties of AIS. The doping of transition metal ions introduces relevant energy levels into the band gap of the crystal, leading to changes in material properties and affecting the performance of the doped QDs [29]. Glutathione (GSH), a tripeptide compound composed of cysteine, glutamic acid, and glycine, contains polar functional groups (carboxyl, amino, sulfhydryl, and amide bonds). These functional groups can interact with the atoms on the surface of AIS QDs through chemical bonding, improving their water solubility and passivating their surface defects.

In this study, Mn-doped AIS quantum dots (Mn-AIS QDs) were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method with GSH acting as a stabilizer. The synthesis process is environmentally friendly and straightforward, yielding samples with stable fluorescence properties. It has been observed that Fe (III) selectively quenches the fluorescence of Mn-AIS QDs, making it suitable for detecting Fe (III) in aqueous environments. By incorporating the reducing agent AA into the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) QDs system, the quenched fluorescence can be revived. This discovery facilitates the development of a fluorescence-based method for detecting AA. Furthermore, Mn-AIS-Fe (III) demonstrates strong anti-interference capabilities, allowing for the precise determination of AA content in vitamin C tablets with dependable results. The fluorescence sensors developed in this study provide several benefits, such as high sensitivity, a broad detection range, a low detection limit, cost-effectiveness, and ease of use.

All chemical reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade without further purification. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), iron(III) trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6) and zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4⋅7H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Indium nitrate hydrate (InN3O9⋅xH2O), glutathione (C10H17N3O6S) and Quinine-sulfate for fluorescence measurement ((C20H24N2O2)2⋅H2SO4) were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S⋅9H2O) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4⋅H2O) was purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. Isopropyl alcohol ((CH3)2CHOH) and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2⋅6H2O) were purchased from Xilong Science. Calcium chloride hexahydrate (CaCl2⋅6H2O) was obtained from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2⋅2H2O) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. Vitamin C tablets was purchased from Huazhong Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water with a specific resistivity greater than 18.25 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiments.

1.00 mL AgNO3 (0.10 mol·L−1), 4.00 mL In(NO3)3·xH2O (0.20 mol·L−1) and 1.00 mL MnSO4 solution (concentration calculated based on the molar ratios of n (Mn): n (Ag + In) = 1:50, 1:100, 1:150, 1:200, 1:300) were sequentially added to a beaker containing 40 mL of deionized water. Stir the mixture for 10 min. Next, add 10.00 mL of GSH solution (0.20 mol·L−1) to the mixture and continue stirring for another 10 min. While stirring, adjust the pH of the mixed solution to 8.50 using NaOH solution (0.50 mol·L−1). Transfer the resulting mixture to a 250 mL three-neck flask fitted with a spherical condensate tube. Heat the mixture in an oil bath to 90 °C under N2 atmosphere with magnetic stirring. Then, quickly add 6.00 mL of Na2S solution (0.10 mol·L−1). Allow the reaction to proceed at 90 °C for 1 h, then stop heating and let it cool naturally to room temperature. Finally, add isopropyl alcohol to the reaction solution for centrifugal washing and precipitation of the QDs (using a volume ratio of 1.5:1). Centrifuge the solution at 9000 r·min−1 for 6 min. Dry the precipitate in a vacuum drying oven and grind it to obtain the QDs. The prepared QDs are labeled as AIS, Mn-AIS (1:50), Mn-AIS (1:100), Mn-AIS (1:150), Mn-AIS (1:200), and Mn-AIS (1:300) respectively.

The samples' crystallinity was determined using X-ray diffractometry (XRD) with a Bruker X-ray powder diffractometer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) technique was employed using an Escalab 250Xi XPS instrument with Al Kα radiation to obtain the binding energies of the constituent elements. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JSM-2100F instrument. The optical absorption capability of the samples was measured using a TU-1901 Double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–Vis, China). Infrared spectra of the AIS and Mn-AIS QDs were obtained using a Nicolet-iS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were performed using Japan's HORIBA FluoroMax-4 instrument. Photoluminescence spectroscopy data was collected using an RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (PL, Shimadzu).

The fluorescence quantum yield of the AIS and Mn-AIS can be calculated using the following Eq. (1) [9]:

where “sam” and “ref” refer to the sample and reference, respectively. In this case, quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 mol·L−1 H2SO4 with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and a quantum yield of 0.54 is chosen as the reference. “I” represents the integrated emission intensity, which can be calculated from the emission spectra obtained at 380 nm excitation. “A” represents the UV–vis absorbance at 380 nm. To avoid re-absorption effects, the absorbance is controlled to be less than 0.1 in a 10 mm quartz absorbance cell. n is the refractive index of the solvent, which is 1.33 for both water and quinine sulfate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative determination of Fe (III) and AA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence measurements were conducted in a 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution medium at room temperature (25 °C). The concentration of the QDs used was 0.16 mg·mL−1. To begin, a 2000 μM Fe (III) solution was prepared. Different volumes of this Fe (III) solution were accurately transferred to 20 mL stopper colorimetric tubes using pipettes. By maintaining a constant volume of 0.16 mg·mL−1 QDs and thorough mixing, a series of Fe (III) solutions with varying concentrations were prepared. After allowing sufficient time for the reaction to occur, the fluorescence intensity was measured, and a linear relationship between the Fe (III) concentration and the quenching capacity was established. The Fe (III) concentration with the highest quenching capacity was selected for the subsequent fluorescence recovery experiment. Next, different volumes of AA were added to create Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA solutions system with various AA concentrations. The solution was thoroughly mixed and incubated for a specific period of time. The fluorescence intensity was then measured, and a linear relationship was established between the AA concentration and the fluorescence recovery amount. All fluorescence measurements were performed under the same conditions: the excitation wavelength was set at 380 nm, and the slit widths were set to EX = 10 nm and EM = 10 nm. The fluorescence intensity was recorded within the wavelength range of 430–740 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the largest emission peak was used for quantitative analysis.

The Mn-AIS QDs, with a final concentration of 0.16 mg·mL−1, were used to assess their selectivity towards various metal ions. Different metal ions, including Al3+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+ and Ba2+, were added to the Mn-AIS QDs solution at a final concentration of 1000 μM, except for Ba2+, which were added at a concentration of 200 μM. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 380 nm to study the selectivity. Furthermore, photoluminescence spectra were recorded for the mixture of Mn-AIS QDs (0.16 mg·mL−1) and Fe (III) (120 μM) in the presence of other substances under identical conditions. Similarly, an interference experiment was conducted to assess the detection of AA by Mn-AIS-Fe (III) in the presence of typical biomolecular substances such as glutathione (GSH), threonine (Thr), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), glucose (Glu), dopamine (DA), S2−, S2O3 2− and other types of vitamins (Folic acid (FA), riboflavin (B2) and Vitamin D (Vd)). Each interfering substance was added at a concentration of 1000 μM, while the concentration of AA was maintained at 100 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe (III) in tap water and AA in vitamin C tablets
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water is obtained directly from the laboratory and filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane before it is used. To treat Vitamin C tablets, 0.10 g of the tablets was grinded in 20 mL of deionized water to obtain a Vitamin C dispersion. Then, centrifuge the mixture at 9000 r·min−1 for 6 min. After that, filter the mixture through a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane and collect the filtrate. The filtrate was diluted 10 times with 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution for future use. For detection, mix a 0.16 mg·mL−1 dispersion solution with 100 μM Fe (III) and 5 mL of the diluted filtrate. All the sensing tests for Fe (III) and AA was performed at a temperature of 25 °C. After a reaction time of 10 min, measure and record the fluorescence spectra of the solutions.

To determine the detection mechanism, the flat band potentials of the Mn-AIS were measured using an electrochemical method. The experiment utilized a three-electrode setup, where the working electrode was an indium tin oxide (ITO) conductive glass coated with the prepared Mn-AIS. The reference electrode used was a saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and the counter electrode was a platinum electrode. The working electrodes were prepared using the Drops of coating method. The ITO conductive glass (1.0 cm × 2.0 cm) was cleaned sequentially in an ultrasonic water bath containing acetone, ethanol, and distilled water for 30 min each, and then dried. A 20 mg sample was dispersed in a solution containing 3.60 mL ethanol and 0.40 mL 5% Nafion using ultrasonication for 30 min, followed by stirring for 4 h. Next, 30 μL of the resulting dispersion was evenly drop-coated onto the conductive surface of the ITO in a 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm area. After standing for 1 h, the working electrode was dried using an infrared lamp. During the experiment, the O2 in the supporting electrolyte was removed by N2 bubbling. Then, the stabilization time of the open circuit potential of the sample was determined. Once the open circuit potential stabilized, the sample was tested using the impedance potential method, and the obtained results were converted into Mott-Schottky curves. Based on the Mott-Schottky curve, the flat band potential of the sample was calculated.

The determination results of the crystal structure, surface elements and binding energy of samples are shown in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1a, different diffraction peaks are identified and indexed with the help of JCPDS Card File NO. 75-0117. The XRD peaks at 27.9°, 46.4° and 51.7° are respectively corresponded to (112), (204) and (312) crystal planes of tetragonal body-centered-phase AIS, respectively [30]. Upon examining the standard cards for Ag2S and In2S3, it was found that Ag2S primarily exists in two crystalline phases: cubic and monoclinic. The cubic phase of Ag2S (JCPDS Card File NO. 76-0134) exhibits its main peak positions at 25.79°, 36.81°, 45.59°, and 53.03°; the monoclinic phase of Ag2S (JCPDS Card File NO. 75-1061) shows its main peak positions at 30.23°, 31.24°, 34.13°, and 37.92°. In2S3 mainly exists in tetragonal and cubic phases. The tetragonal phase of In2S3 (JCPDS Card File NO. 73-1366) has the main peak positions at 27.41°, 33.21°, and 47.67°; the cubic phase of In2S3, indexed as JCPDS Card File NO. 71-1813, displays its main peak positions at 25.61°, 27.16°, 37.68°, 41.29°, and 47.99°. The characteristic peaks contributed by impurities such as Ag2S or In2S3 are not observed, which demonstrates the high quality of the as-prepared AIS QDs. The position of the diffraction peak generated after Mn ions doping is the same as AIS, indicating that Mn ions doping does not change the unit cell parameters of the crystal structure of AIS. It can be observed that the diffraction peak at 27.9° is sharper in different proportions of Mn-AIS compared to AIS, indicating that Mn ions doping leads to better crystallinity of AIS. In all cases, no diffraction peaks belonging to a piece of Mn element phase are observed, which proves that doped Mn ions is uniformly distributed in the AIS and forms Mn-AIS with high structural purity.

The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 1b) validates the existence of Ag, In, S, C and O elements in the prepared AIS, a small Mn peak is observed in Mn-AIS (1:150). The signals of C and O in the XPS survey spectrum come from glutathione. Therefore, it can be inferred that Ag, In, and S are the main elements of AIS, while Ag, In, S, and Mn are the main elements of Mn-AIS (1:150). Fig. 1c shows that the high-resolution XPS spectra of Mn 2p. For Mn-AIS (1:150), the binding energy curve of Mn 2p is noisy due to the low content. The fitting peaks located at 638.8 eV and 649.9 eV are assigned to the Mn2+, 641.4 eV and 642.8 eV are assigned to the Mn3+ [31,32]. In order to clearly observe the binding energy peaks of Mn 2p, XPS measurement of Mn-AIS (1:50) with the highest Mn content was carried out, as shown in Fig. S1. From Fig. S1b, it can be observed that the fitting peak for Mn2+ is located at 639.2 eV, while those for Mn3+ are at 640.6 eV and 641.9 eV. Compared with those in Mn-AIS (1:150) and Mn-AIS (1:50), Mn 2p in AIS showed no significant binding energy peaks. The actual content of Mn element measured by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES) in Table 1 shows close proximity to the theoretical value, indicating successful doping of Mn ions into AIS. From Fig. 1d, it can be observed that the binding energy peaks of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 are 373.7 eV and 367.7 eV in AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150), which well all agree with the characteristic binding energy spectrum of Ag+ [33,34]. Each group of spin–orbit components (Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2) was separated by a binding energy interval of approximately 6.0 eV, characteristic of silver [35]. Fig. 1e shows that the binding energy peaks of In 3d3/2 and In 3d5/2 in AIS are 451.9 and 444.4 eV, while in Mn-AIS are 452.0 eV and 444.5 eV, which are all the same characteristic binding energy spectrum of In3+ [36]. Comparing with the binding energy peaks of In 3d3/2 and In 3d5/2 in AIS, the binding ability of In3+ to electrons in the crystal lattice is enhanced after the introduction of Mn ions. It is noteworthy that the binding energy peaks of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 in Mn-AIS (1:150) are not changed in position, indicating that Mn ion replaces In3+ in the lattice followed by increase of the binding energy of In3+. Fig. 1f shows the binding energy peaks of S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2. After peak fitting, the binding energies are located at 162.2 and 161.2 eV in AIS, 162.3 and 161.2 eV in Mn-AIS (1:150), respectively, indicating that the S element in the samples is all in the electronic state S2− [37,38]. It can be observed that the binding ability of S is slightly enhanced after the introduction of Mn ions, forming Mn-S bond. For the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1g), it can be observed that three peaks are located at 531.5, 533.3 and 535.6 eV in AIS, 531.3, 532.8 and 535.4 eV in Mn-AIS (1:150), which are related to C=O, hydroxyls of adsorbed water and oxygen molecules, respectively [39]. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1h) presents three peaks at 284.6, 286.0 and 288.1 eV in AIS, 284.7, 285.9 and 287.9 eV in Mn-AIS (1:150), which could be ascribed to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–O–C and C=O, respectively [40]. Thus, the analysis of XPS shows that the valence states of Ag, In and S in the prepared sample are +1, +3 and −2, respectively. Meanwhile, the doping of Mn ions did not change the oxidation state of the three elements. When Mn ion is introduced into AIS, it undergoes a change in valence state and exists in both the +2 and +3 valence states. The Mn2+/3+ ions then replace a portion of the In3+ ions within the lattice structure, forming In-S and Mn-S bonds. The binding energy of In and S elements moves to the direction of high binding energy, indicating that electron transfer occurs.

HRTEM images of as-prepared AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150) were shown in Fig. 2 . From Fig. 2a and b, it can be observed that the prepared AIS nanoparticles exhibit good dispersibility and a spherical morphology, with a size range of approximately 5–15 nm. The interplanar spacing measured from Fig. 2c is 0.33 nm, which corresponds to the (112) crystal plane of tetragonal phase AIS. Compared to AIS, as shown in Fig. 2d and e, Mn-AIS (1:150) also exhibits good dispersibility and a spherical morphology, while it has a smoother surface and a particle size of about 10 nm. The lattice stripes can be observed clearly from Fig. 2f, with crystal plane spacing of 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (112) crystal plane of the tetragonal phase AIS.

The FT-IR spectra of GSH, AIS, and Mn-AIS (1:150) are shown in Fig. 3 . In the FT-IR spectrum of GSH, 3347 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of –OH, 3251 cm−1 is the characteristic peak of –NH, and 3129 cm−1 and 3026 cm−1 belong to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of –NH peak (amphoteric ion NH3+). 2524 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of −SH peak, 1713 cm−1 is attributed to C=O, the presence of the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the COO– group at 1599 cm−1 and 1394 cm−1, and the peak at 1073 cm−1 is the result of C–N stretching vibration [41]. The characteristic band at 2524 cm−1 is caused by S-H tensile vibration [42]. These groups disappear or weaken in the spectra of AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150), indicating that these groups in GSH successfully bind to the surface of AIS and Mn-AIS. The disappearance of S-H tensile band indicates that AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150) form complexes with GSH through In-S interaction. These results indicate that hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino functional groups generated by glutathione bond to the surface of QDs. Therefore, the prepared QDs have good water solubility. When stored in air for several months away from light, the water solution of QDs remained transparent, and no significant precipitation was observed.

AIS and Mn-AIS QDs exhibit excellent absorption and emission properties. Fig. 4 shows the UV–visible absorption and photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of AIS and Mn-AIS. The absorption spectra do not exhibit well-defined exciton absorption peaks in the long-wavelength spectral region, possibly due to a wide size distribution, trap-state-associated emissions, or a combination of both [43]. A small bend at around 300 nm can be observed, a characteristic feature of chalcopyrite QDs solutions. The long tail in the absorption spectra of chalcopyrite QDs is commonly explained by size and shape inhomogeneity of the QDs, as well as optical transitions via intra-band gap states in the QDs. The PL peaks of AIS show a slight blue shift from 575 to 560 nm upon doping with Mn ions. This blue shift may be attributed to cation exchange occurring in the QDs core, resulting in simultaneous shifts in absorption and PL spectra [44,45]. Comparatively, a noticeable blue shift is observed in the absorption spectrum compared to the PL peak position, believed to be due to exciton energy loss through phonon excitation before decay [46]. The PL of I–III–VI QDs arises from electron-hole pair emissions in the defect state [47]. It is noted that the conduction band In AIS semiconductor is composed of hybridized In 5s and 5p orbitals with S 3p, while the valence band is formed by hybrid orbitals of S 3p and Ag 4d [48]. The substantial Stokes shift indicates that the observed PL is not a band gap emission but rather emissions from surface states and intrinsic states [43]. Ternary I–III–VI semiconductors are known for their abundance of trap states, which can involve gap atoms or vacancies and are responsible for donor–acceptor transitions. The maximum emission wavelength of the PL peak of Mn-AIS (1:150) is 566 nm, where the fluorescence intensity reaches its maximum at this doping ratio. After doping with different proportions of Mn, the change in fluorescence intensity of the AIS QDs measured shows no regularity. As the amount of Mn introduced increases in the order of 1:300, 1:200, 1:150, 1:100, 1:50, the fluorescence intensity first increases, then decreases, increases again (reaching the highest point), decreases once more, and shows another increase at the 1:50 ratio. Nevertheless, despite this, the fluorescence intensity of the Mn-doped AIS QDs is significantly stronger compared to the undoped AIS QDs. Based on the test results of XRD, XPS, and HRTEM, we speculate that the crystallinity of Mn-doped AIS QDs improves after doping with Mn ions. The binding ability of S and In to electrons increases, the surface of AIS QDs becomes smoother, surface defects decrease, non-radiative recombination of excitons reduces, resulting in enhanced fluorescence. As for the randomness observed in the influence of different Mn doping ratios on the fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs, we also suspect that the irregular enhancement of fluorescence was due to the inability to proportionally dope as per the feeding ratios of 1:300, 1:200, 1:150, 1:100, 1:50. We tested the Mn content in five samples using ICP-OES technology (see Table 1), and the results showed that the order of Mn content in the five samples matched the feeding ratios consistently. Therefore, we speculate that the irregular variations in fluorescence intensity of AIS QDs with different Mn doping ratios may be attributed to the difficulty in controlling non-radiative recombination caused by surface defects. Additionally, the PL quantum yield (PLQY) of AIS QDs in aqueous solution was determined as 9.4% using a fluorescence spectrometer, while the Mn-AIS (1:150) quantum yield increased to about 25% (Table S1). The digital photograph displayed in Fig. 4 shows Mn-AIS (1:150) dispersed in water under daylight (left) and ultraviolet light (right). It can be observed that Mn-AIS QDs emits a vibrant yellow fluorescence upon excitation at 365 nm, which is consistent with the results of the fluorescence measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transient fluorescence of AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150)
ce:section.ce:para:
The PL emission of AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs was measured with excitation wavelengths of 380 nm. AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs exhibited a maximum emission wavelength of 566 nm. The fluorescence decay curves were well-fitted by a double-exponential function [46]:
(2)

where I(t) is the PL intensity at delay time (t), τ 1 and τ 2 represents the decay time of PL intensity, and B 1 and B 2 represent the weight coefficients of τ 1 and τ 2. The faster decay time (τ 1) component is due to the nuclear state of initial filling and the intrinsic recombination process of surface defect states, while the slower decay time (τ 2) is due to the radiative recombination process of donor–acceptor pairs or deep defection-related recombination mechanisms. Fig. S2 shows the transient fluorescence spectra of AIS and Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs, and Table S2 shows the specific values of various indices corresponding to the decay curve. According to Table S2, it can be observed that for AIS QDs, the fast decay index τ1 dominates a significant portion of the emission spectrum, accounting for 55.0%, while the slow decay index τ2 associated with the main fluorescence emission pathway only represents 45.0%. This is attributed to the presence of numerous lattice defects (including internal and surface defects) in AIS QDs caused by their imperfect crystal structure, leading to the loss of composite electrons [43]. Consequently, the transient fluorescence spectrum exhibits a relatively rapid attenuation curve, as depicted in Fig. S2. In contrast, Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs exhibit an increased proportion of the fast decay index τ1 in the entire emission spectrum (61.5%), accompanied by a lower contribution from the slow attenuation index τ2 (38.5%). As a result, the average lifetime of the QDs has been extended from 168.68 ns to 185.51 ns. This phenomenon indicates that the introduction of Mn2+ into the Ag-In-S structure to form Ag-In-Mn-S, which can improve the internal defect structure of QDs and prevent the occurrence of non-radiative recombination process to a certain extent. Mn-AIS (1:150) material with long lifetime has significant advantages for fluorescence sensing applications.

The fluorescence spectra of Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs at different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. S3a. As the excitation wavelength increases from 310 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity of the emission peak at 566 nm initially increases and then decreases. The maximum fluorescence intensity is observed when the excitation wavelength is 380 nm. Therefore, subsequent experiments were conducted using an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Fig. S3b shows the excitation spectra and emission spectra of Mn-AIS (1:150) (excitation wavelength is 380 nm), and the peak position of the excitation spectra is about 380 nm, corresponding to the results in Fig. S3a. In Fig. S3c, the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS (1:150) was measured at different temperatures. It was found that Mn-AIS (1:150) exhibits temperature dependence, with the fluorescence intensity gradually decreasing as the temperature increases. This decrease in fluorescence intensity can be attributed to non-radiative recombination of excitons caused by internal energy conversion and thermal activation of surface traps within the molecule [49]. It is evident that temperature significantly influences the fluorescence intensity. Based on the actual measurement conditions, a temperature of 25 °C was selected for subsequent fluorescence measurements.

Furthermore, the impact of pH value on the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs was examined, and the findings are illustrated in Fig. S4a. Considering that deionized water lacks buffering capacity, the addition of analytes or other interferents during the detection process may alter the pH value, preventing the system from maintaining a pH of 7.00. Therefore, a buffer solution is employed for the experiment. In this experiment, a 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution was used, and 0.10 mol·L−1 NaOH or 0.10 mol·L−1 phosphoric acid was employed to adjust the pH of the solution. It was noted that the QDs demonstrated optimal fluorescence intensity within a pH range of 5.00 to 10.00. This can be attributed to the deprotonation of the carboxyl group in Mn-AIS under alkaline conditions, resulting in a negatively charged surface on Mn-AIS (1:150) QDs. The repulsion between charges hinders aggregation and enhances fluorescence intensity by promoting smoother electron migration. In weakly acidic to neutral environments, the fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced. However, in strongly acidic solutions, the QDs experienced a sharp decline in fluorescence intensity. This is due to the partial protonation of carboxyl groups, which reduces the charge on the QDs, leading to destabilization of Mn-AIS and a roughened surface that impedes electron migration, thereby diminishing the fluorescence of QDs. As shown in Fig. S4b, the quenching efficiency reaches its maximum at a pH of 8.00 in the buffer solution. Therefore, subsequent experiments for fluorescence measurement are conducted using a 0.10 mol·L−1 buffer solution with a pH of 8.00.

To investigate the effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs when exposed to Fe (III), we performed an experiment that compared the fluorescence intensity at various time points following the addition of two distinct concentrations of Fe (III) (10 μM and 50 μM). The results are illustrated in Fig. S5a. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs decreased rapidly within 5 min and reached a relatively stable level after 10 min. Based on these findings, we determined that the optimal incubation time for the experiment is 10 min. Furthermore, we also investigated the incubation time required for fluorescence recovery upon adding AA. The plot of fluorescence intensity versus incubation time is shown in Fig. S5b. It was discovered that the fluorescence gradually recovered upon addition of AA and reached equilibrium after 10 min. Therefore, we considered this 10-minute duration as the equilibrium time for fluorescence recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of Mn-AIS fluorescence by Fe (III)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 5 a shows the fluorescence spectra of Mn-AIS QDs solution excited at 380 nm in the presence of Fe (III) (0–120 μM), the PL intensity decreased almost linearly with increasing concentration of Fe (III) (0–120 µM) in 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution at pH = 8.0. The fluorescence could be quenched gradually with increasing the concentration of Fe (III). The quenching amount ΔF, ΔF=F 0-F, increased with increasing the concentration of Fe (III) ions (Fig. 5b) and displayed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.03–0.12 µM and 0.12–120 µM with a linear correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9997 and 0.9974 (Fig. 5b), respectively, where F 0 is the fluorescence intensity of the initial Mn-AIS QDs solution, and F is the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs solution containing different concentration of Fe (III). According to Eqs. (4) and (5), the LOD of Fe (III) is 0.16 nM, and the LOQ is 0.55 nM.
(4)
LOD =
3 δ
s
(5)
LOQ =
10 δ
s
where δ represents the standard deviation of the blank solution, and s is the slope of the linear equation. Table 2 presents a comparison of the method developed in this study with other reported methods for Fe (III) detection. The analytical performance metrics, including sensitivity and linear range, demonstrate either comparable or superior results, suggesting the potential for promising applications in environmental systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The recovery of the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) fluorescence by AA
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of AA concentration on the fluorescence spectra of the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system was investigated under optimized conditions. Fig. 6 a illustrates the impact of AA concentration on the fluorescence recovery of the system within a 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution at pH = 8.00. It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of the system increases with the increase of AA concentration. The amount of fluorescence recovery ΔF', ΔF'=F'-F 0′, was used to indicate the fluorescence recovery capacity of AA. Here, F' represents the recovery fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS-Fe (III) in the presence of AA, and F 0′ is the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS in the presence of Fe (III) (120 μM). Fig. 6b shows the standard curve of ΔF' versus AA concentration C in a 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution at pH = 8.00. The enhancement of fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of AA in the range of 0.05–180 μM (R2 = 0.9965). The LOD for AA was determined to be 0.031 μM, and the LOQ was calculated as 0.11 μM based on three times the standard deviation rule. Table 3 compares the method developed in this study with other reported methods for AA detection. In comparison to the reported methods, the approach established herein also demonstrates a broad detection range and a low detection limit.

The stability of the probe is undoubtedly a crucial factor in establishing a sensing platform. In this study, we investigated the effects of storage time, salinity conditions, visible light irradiation, and natural light irradiation on the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs solution. As shown in Fig. S6a, after 30 days of storage, the Mn-AIS QDs solution remained transparent and clear, with a consistent color similar to that of the freshly prepared solution. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the Mn-AIS QDs solution remained unchanged (Fig. S6b). We also investigated the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs under varying NaCl concentrations (0–100 μM). Interestingly, the presence of environmental ions had a minimal effect on the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs. The Mn-AIS QDs displayed robust and stable fluorescence performance across a range of NaCl concentrations, indicating excellent salt resistance (Fig. S6c). When subjected to continuous irradiation for 120 min from a 1000 W tungsten halide lamp (with a 420 nm filter to block light below 420 nm) and sunlight, as shown in Fig. S6d, the fluorescence intensity of Mn-AIS QDs barely changed, demonstrating the QDs' good fluorescence stability and excellent light resistance. Based on these findings, it is clear that the established sensing platform exhibits remarkable stability and can be effectively employed for sensor analysis under complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference for Fe (III) detecting
ce:section.ce:para: To ensure accurate results, it is important to study the potential influence of various ions, particularly cations, on Fe (III) ion sensing in tap water. Therefore, we prepared seven different aqueous solutions of QDs containing different ions (Al3+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Zn2+, Ba2+). The concentrations of Ba2+ were 200 µM, while the other ion concentrations were 1000 µM, allowing us to assess sensing selectivity and interference. The selectivity of Mn-AIS QDs towards various metal ions was evaluated using a final concentration of 0.16 mg·mL−1. Fig. 7 a shows that the FL intensity significantly decreases with the addition of Fe (III), while other ions have minimal or negligible impact on the PL intensity. This indicates that the Mn-AIS QDs exhibit high selectivity towards Fe (III) ions. When other ions are added to the Mn-AIS system including 120 μM Fe (III) (represents as Mn-AIS-Fe (III)), the impact of other ions on the photoluminescence (PL) intensity is negligible (Fig. 7b). The results above indicate that Mn-AIS QDs exhibits excellent selectivity and resistance to interference for the detection of Fe (III) in a 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution at pH = 8.00.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference for AA detecting
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the fluorescence recovery capacity of AA, various biomolecules and ions (at a concentration of 1000 µM), including Threonine (Thr), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), dopamine (DA), S2−, S2O3 2−, Folic acid (FA), riboflavin (B2) and Vitamin D (Vd), were individually added to the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system. The correlation between the relative fluorescence intensity F/F 0 (where F and F 0 represent the PL intensity of Mn-AIS-Fe (III) in the presence and absence of the aforementioned biomolecules or ions, respectively) and these species is depicted in Fig. 8 a. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Observations indicated that the fluorescence recovery degree of 1000 µM carbohydrates and non-mercaptan amino acids was significantly lower than that of 100 µM AA. However, the fluorescence of Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system could be partially restored by 1000 µM GSH, DA, B2, and Vd. This restoration may be due to the coordination ability of the surface groups of these substances with Fe (III). After coordinating with these groups, Fe (III) dissociates from the surface of Mn-AIS QDs, resulting in fluorescence recovery of Mn-AIS QDs, however, this effect is less pronounced than with AA. Under the same conditions, an interference test was conducted to evaluate coexistence with other substances. In the presence of 100 µM AA, 1000 µM of interfering substances were introduced to the Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA system. As shown in Fig. 8b, these interfering substances had minimal impact on the fluorescence recovery of Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA system. The effect of B2 is slightly more pronounced, possibly because B2 itself possesses fluorescence recovery properties, and its recovery capability is enhanced when combined with AA. The results above demonstrate that the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system exhibits a certain degree of selectivity and anti-interference capability for the detection of AA.

To assess the stability and repeatability of the fluorescence probe, we conducted measurements of the fluorescence intensity for 10 samples each of 20 µM Fe (III) solution and 10 µM AA solution, respectively. The precision experimental results are summarized in Tables S3 and S4. For the Fe (III) solution, the mean value was determined to be 19.96 µM, with a standard deviation (SD) of 0.2332 and a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.2%. For the AA solution, the mean concentration was found to be 10.03 µM, with an SD of 0.0375 and an RSD of 0.4%. The calculated relative standard deviations for both Fe (III) and AA were below 2%, indicating that the established method demonstrates good repeatability and stability, as evidenced by the low variability observed in the measurements.

The method established in this experiment is satisfactory under the condition of 0.10 mol·L−1 citrate-disodium hydrogen phosphate buffer solution. As indicated in Table 4 , the recoveries of Fe (III) in tap water ranged from 97.7 to 100.2%, with an RSD of 0.40–4.51%. In order to validate the accuracy and precision of the Mn-AIS QDs-based PL sensor, the same experiments were conducted using the ICP-OES method. The results by the ICP-OES method reveal that the average recoveries ranged from 101.8% to 118.3%, with an RSD values less than 1.0% (n = 3). In comparison to the results derived from ICP-OES, the analytical outcomes of the sensor showed a strong alignment with the conventional ICP-OES technique. Furthermore, the detection process as a whole was notably more user-friendly and expeditious. Data presented in Table 5 reveals that the recovery efficiency of AA in Vitamin C tablets spanned from 100.3% to 103.7%, accompanied by an RSD ranging from 0.13% to 2.52%. The results underscore the reliability and appropriateness of the proposed fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe (III) and AA in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the detection of Fe (III) and AA using Mn-AIS QDs fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching is caused by the interaction between fluorescent molecules and inhibitory molecules and is usually divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching. Static quenching refers to the process in which the quencher reacts with the ground state molecule of the fluorescent substance to produce the non-luminous substance, thus reducing the fluorescence intensity. Dynamic quenching refers to the process in which the fluorescence intensity decreases due to the collision between the quencher and the excited molecules of the fluorescent substance. To understand the quenching mechanism by Fe (III) and the fluorescence recovery mechanism by AA, the temperature dependent fluorescence detection for Fe (III), UV–vis absorbance spectroscopy, FT-IR spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime decays, and the band structure were studied.

Generally, the quenching of the fluorophores can be realized through dynamic quenching effect or static quenching effect or both of them. Both models can be described in theory by Stern-Volmer equation (6):
(6)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
Q
where F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore in the absence and presence of the quencher respectively; [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher and K sv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

Fig. 9 a displays the fitted Stern-Volmer curves at different temperatures. The specific parameters were presented in Table S5. The results showed that the quenching constants are 0.0740, 0.0775 and 0.1119 L⋅μmol−1 at temperatures of 303.15 K, 313.15 K and 323.15 K, respectively. Evidently, the quenching constant increases as the temperature rises, aligning with the attributes of dynamic quenching.

The UV–visible absorption spectra of Mn-AIS, Mn-AIS-Fe (III), Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA, Fe (III) and AA are shown in Fig. 9b. It can be observed that Fe (III) exhibits absorption in the range of 200–400 nm. When Fe (III) ions are added to Mn-AIS, the resulting mixture shows enhanced absorption but no new peaks, indicating that no chemical reaction occurs between them, thus, fluorescence quenching of Mn-AIS by Fe (III) is a dynamic quenching process, which is consistent with the fitting result of Stern-Volmer equation. AA exhibits the strongest absorption at 265 nm. When AA is added to Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system, the resulting mixture exhibits an absorption peak at 255 nm. The appearance of this new peak indicates a chemical reaction between AA and Mn-AIS- Fe (III), resulting in the oxidation of AA. This result suggests that the fluorescence recovery of Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system by AA is achieved through a chemical reaction between AA and Mn-AIS- Fe (III).

The FT-IR spectra of Mn-AIS QDs, Mn-AIS-Fe (III) and Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA are displayed in Fig. 9c. The FT-IR spectra of Mn-AIS QDs and Mn-AIS-Fe (III) are nearly identical, except for the peak at 1394 cm−1, which moves slightly towards the long wavenumber direction. As the peak at 1394 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration of COO, this implies a strong interaction between Fe (III) and the COO group of Mn-AIS QDs. Similarly, the FTIR spectrum of Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA also bears resemblance to that of Mn-AIS-Fe (III), with the important distinction that the peak at 1394 cm−1 has returned to its original position, which suggest that when Fe (III) combines with COO, AA enables the separation of Fe (III) from the carboxyl group, thereby facilitating fluorescence recovery.

The fluorescence lifetime study serves as a valuable method for distinguishing between dynamic quenching and static quenching. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements (Fig. 9d and Table S6) reveal that the average fluorescence lifetime of Mn-AIS is 185.51 ns, which decreases to 146.45 ns for Mn-AIS-Fe (III). The pronounced alteration in fluorescence lifetime suggests that the quenching of Mn-AIS QDs fluorescence induced by Fe (III) is attributed to dynamic quenching. The fluorescence lifetime of Mn-AIS-Fe (III)-AA recovers to 223.62 ns, indicating that Fe dissociates from the surface of the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) due to the addition of AA.

Dynamic quenching was caused by charge transfer or energy transfer. In order to accurately determine which dynamic quenching occurs, fluorescence spectra of Mn-AIS and UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe (III) were analyzed. As shown in Fig. 9e, there is no overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of Mn-AIS and the absorption spectrum of Fe (III), thus the possibility of energy transfer quenching is lost. Thus, it is concluded that Fe (III) quenching of Mn-AIS fluorescence occurs in the form of charge transfer. To further understand this process, we studied the optical bandgap (E g) and band edge positions of Mn-AIS (Fig. 9f and g). Based on the UV–vis absorption spectrum, the bandgap of Mn-AIS is about 2.00 eV by using a Tauc plot (Fig. 9f). Mott-Schottky curve of Mn-AIS was tested by the impedance potential method, as shown in Fig. 9g. The tangent slope of Mn-AIS is positive, indicating that the Mn-AIS is n-type semiconductor. According to the intercept of the straight lines on the x-axis, the flat band potential is −0.94 V (vs SCE) for Mn-AIS. In n-type semiconductors, the conduction band potential is approximately equal to the flat band potential, and the valence band potential is calculated according to the empirical formula (7):
(7)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g
where E VB , E CB , and E g represent valence band (VB) potential, conduction band (CB) potential, and band gap energy, respectively. Thereby, the calculated valence band potential of Mn-AIS is 1.06 V (vs SCE). The electrode potential of Fe3+/Fe2+ is 0.53 eV (vs SCE), Fe3+/Fe is −0.28 V (vs SCE), and Fe2+/Fe is −0.68 V (vs SCE), all situated between CB and VB of Mn-AIS. Therefore, when Mn-AIS was irradiated, the electrons of VB were excited to CB and further transferred to the Fe species, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. Hence, Fe (III) can quench the fluorescence of Mn-AIS through the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism.

Based on the above results, a possible detection mechanism for Fe (III) and AA based on Mn-AIS QDs fluorescence sensor was proposed, as schemed in Fig. 10 . The prepared Mn-AIS ODs, stabilized by GSH, possess a high density of functional groups on their surface, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups. Under excitation at 380 nm, the strongest fluorescence emission peak of Mn-AIS QDs is observed at 566 nm. Upon the addition of Fe (III), the strong interaction between Fe (III) and carboxyl groups leads to the binding of Fe (III) to the surface of Mn-AIS QDs. When Mn-AIS is excited by 380 nm light, the generated electrons rapidly transfer to Fe (III) due to the tight contact, Fe3+ captures the excited electrons, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of Mn-AIS QDs. Upon the addition of AA into the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system, the strong reducing ability of AA (The redox potential of AA/DHA (Dehydroascorbic acid). is −0.18 eV (vs SCE)) triggers a reduction reaction with Fe3+, leading to the detachment of Fe3+ from the surface of Mn-AIS QDs. Consequently, the fluorescence of Mn-AIS-Fe (III) is restored.

In summary, we have successfully prepared Mn-AIS QDs using a low-temperature liquid phase method. These QDs can serve as an efficient “on-off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe (III) and AA. By introducing Mn ions into the AIS lattice and partially substituting In3+, we were able to improve the defects in the AIS lattice, resulting in Mn-AIS exhibiting a higher fluorescence quantum yield (25%) compared to AIS (9.4%) when excited at a wavelength of 380 nm. The fluorescence of Mn-AIS can be quenched by Fe (III), as it forms a complex with Mn-AIS through the oxygen-containing functional groups. This quenching process follows the PET mechanism. Upon the addition of AA to the Mn-AIS-Fe (III) system, the fluorescence is recovered. This is because AA has strong reduced ability and can react with Fe (III), causing Fe (III) to dissociate from the surface of Mn-AIS. Furthermore, our sensor platform demonstrates high selectivity and sensitivity in detecting Fe (III) in tap water and AA in vitamin C tablets. This strategy is simple, cost-effective, and holds great potential for applications in the development of novel biological and environmental sensors.
